Entity_Description: Logan Lake is a district municipality in the Thompson-Nicola region of British Columbia's Interior Plateau, roughly 39 kilometres southwest of Kamloops. Established in 1970 as a planned residential community for workers at the nearby Highland Valley Copper mine, Canada's largest open-pit copper operation, it recorded a population of 2,255 in the 2021 Census. Sitting at about 1,100 metres elevation among lakes and forest, the town is nicknamed the Home of the Giant Truck for the retired haul truck displayed at its centre.

Our {BUSINESS_TYPE} crews know the area's 1970s-planned subdivisions, lakefront properties, and snowy high-elevation winters, delivering {SERVICE_CATEGORY} built for real local conditions. Call {PHONE}.
Short_Description: Logan Lake exists because of copper. The town was carved out of the Interior Plateau forest in 1970 to house workers at Highland Valley Copper, Canada's largest open-pit mine just up the road, and today a retired haul truck still stands guard at its centre, earning the Home of the Giant Truck nickname. That mining backbone, plus lakefront cabins and 1970s subdivisions perched near 1,100 metres, shapes the {SERVICE} local property owners need. {COMPANY_NAME} brings {BUSINESS_TYPE} expertise tuned to Logan Lake's snowy winters, dry summers, and shift-work schedules, delivering {SERVICE_CATEGORY} that holds up to real interior conditions. Whether you own a home in the townsite or an acreage toward Highland Valley, we treat every job like our reputation depends on it. Call {PHONE}.
Long_Description:
Few towns wear their origins as plainly as Logan Lake. When Highland Valley Copper needed a place for its workforce in 1970, planners laid out an entire community on the forested Interior Plateau, and that deliberate beginning still defines the place. Drive through the townsite today and you find blocks of uniform 1970s housing arranged around loops and cul-de-sacs, edged by manufactured homes and lakefront cabins along the water. For a {BUSINESS_TYPE}, that mix matters: a compact planned subdivision, an older acreage, and a seasonal cabin each ask something different from {SERVICE}, and {COMPANY_NAME} tailors {SERVICE_CATEGORY} to each one.

Elevation is the second thing that shapes work here. At roughly 1,100 metres, Logan Lake sits well above the valley floor at Kamloops, 39 kilometres northeast, so it trades some of the region's blistering summer heat for colder, snowier winters and hard freeze-thaw cycles. Dry interior summers still bring grassland wildfire risk, but it is snow load, frost, and sharp seasonal swings that most often test local properties. {COMPANY_NAME} plans every {SERVICE} visit around that high-country reality rather than treating Logan Lake like the warmer towns downhill.

The economy follows the mine. Highland Valley Copper, Canada's largest open-pit copper operation, remains the anchor employer, and its shift schedules ripple through the community, from when residents are home to how they budget. {COMPANY_NAME} works around those rotations, offering {ESTIMATE_TYPE} and flexible scheduling so a job never collides with a shift change. That mining identity is stamped on the town itself: the retired haul truck at its heart earned Logan Lake the Home of the Giant Truck nickname and remains its best-known landmark.

Coverage reaches beyond the townsite too. From Logan Lake, {COMPANY_NAME} serves the surrounding Thompson-Nicola communities, including Ashcroft, Merritt about 43 kilometres south, and Kamloops, plus properties strung along the route toward the Highland Valley operation. Whether you own a lakefront cabin, a townsite home, or a rural acreage on the plateau, {UNIQUE_VALUE_PROP} backs every job. Reach {COMPANY_NAME} at {PHONE} to book {SERVICE} tuned to the way Logan Lake actually lives and works.
Local_Facts:
- Population: 2,255 (2021 Census)
- Founded in 1970 as a planned community for Highland Valley Copper mine workers
- Highland Valley Copper, near Logan Lake, is Canada's largest open-pit copper mine
- Nicknamed the Home of the Giant Truck for the retired haul truck displayed in town
- Located about 39 km southwest of Kamloops in the Thompson-Nicola region
- Sits at roughly 1,100 metres elevation on BC's Interior Plateau
- FSA V0K; part of the Thompson-Nicola Regional District
